Construct: The degree to which a brand surfaces in a consumer's mind unprompted within a category-relevant context. Reflects the strength and accessibility of brand-associated memory structures (Keller 1993; Romaniuk & Sharp 2004). Measures how easily and quickly the brand is retrieved relative to retrieval effort expended.
Linguistic Surface: References to the brand appearing without elicitation, low-effort recall language ('just came to mind', 'first thing I thought of', 'didn't have to search'), or the brand being defaulted to in category-neutral prompts.
Boundary: Not Brand Mention Share (which is a corpus-level volumetric measure across many utterances). Not Category Salience (where the category, not the brand, is the priming cue). Not Brand Recall Language (which is about how the brand is referred to descriptively, not about retrieval ease).
Theoretical Anchor: Keller (1993) CBBE; Romaniuk & Sharp (2004) mental availability; Ehrenberg-Bass Institute
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Recall Language, Category Salience, Brand Discovery
Example 1: I didn't even have to think about it — the name just popped into my head.
Example 2: Whenever someone asks me for a recommendation, this brand is always the first one I mention.
Example 3: Their name is just stuck in my head at this point.

Construct: The strength of the bidirectional cognitive association between a brand and its product category — such that the category cues the brand and vice versa. A high-category-salience brand is treated as a default exemplar of its category (Nedungadi 1990; Rosch's prototype theory).
Linguistic Surface: Utterances where the category is the prompt and the brand emerges as the answer: 'when I think of X, I think of [Brand]', 'they basically ARE the category', '[Category] = [Brand] for me'. Also brand-as-noun usage ('I need to get a [Brand]' for a category item).
Boundary: Differs from Brand Salience in directionality: Brand Salience is about the brand surfacing in any context; Category Salience requires the category to be the cue. Differs from Category Leadership Signals (Competitive Energy), which assert market position rather than mental association.
Theoretical Anchor: Nedungadi (1990) category accessibility; Rosch (1978) prototype theory; Keller (1993)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Salience, Market Leader Benchmarking, Category Leadership Signals
Example 1: When you say EV, this brand is basically what comes to mind first.
Example 2: For headphones in this price range, they're THE name.
Example 3: It's like saying 'Xerox' — the brand has just become the category.

Construct: A first-encounter event in which the consumer becomes aware of the brand for the first time. Marks the entry point of the funnel and the moment of category-set expansion (Howard & Sheth 1969).
Linguistic Surface: Explicit novelty markers attached to the brand: 'just discovered', 'never heard of them until', 'someone introduced me to', 'first time seeing this brand', 'where has this been all my life'.
Boundary: Not Product Curiosity (which presupposes awareness and asks deeper questions). Not Brand Salience (which assumes the brand is already in memory). Discovery is the *moment* of entry into awareness.
Theoretical Anchor: Howard & Sheth (1969) Theory of Buyer Behaviour; Lavidge & Steiner (1961) hierarchy of effects
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Product Curiosity, Awareness & Salience Signals, Word-of-mouth Promotion
Example 1: Just stumbled on this brand through an Instagram ad — never heard of them before.
Example 2: A colleague mentioned them last week; I had no idea this brand even existed.
Example 3: Where has this been all my life? Just discovered them yesterday.

Construct: The descriptive vocabulary consumers use to refer to a brand when its exact name is not immediately available — proxy references that index how the brand is stored in memory (visual, functional, categorical, or social associations).
Linguistic Surface: Descriptive substitutes for the brand name: 'the one with the [colour] logo', 'that brand from the [ad/influencer]', 'the [adjective] one', 'you know — the one that does [X]'.
Boundary: Not Brand Salience (which is about retrieval ease, not retrieval form). Not Tribe Language (which is in-group jargon among existing users). This signal is specifically about *imperfect retrieval* that still successfully points to the brand.
Theoretical Anchor: Keller (1993) brand associations; Krishnan (1996) brand image memory structure
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Salience, Brand Positioning Language
Example 1: Which one was that again? The one with the blue logo and the weird ads?
Example 2: You know, the brand that always sponsors the cricket — yeah, that one.
Example 3: What's it called... the cheap-looking one with the funny mascot.

Construct: The relative proportion of brand references in a given discourse corpus or thread compared to competing brands. A corpus-level (not utterance-level) signal that indexes share-of-voice within a conversational context.
Linguistic Surface: Observer-style utterances that quantify or qualify brand presence in a discussion: 'most people here are mentioning [Brand]', '8 out of 10 comments are about [Brand]', 'almost no one is talking about [competitor]'.
Boundary: Not Brand Salience (individual-level retrieval). Not Demand Momentum (which describes change over time, not snapshot share). This signal is meta-observational — the speaker is reporting on a conversational pattern rather than expressing their own brand pull.
Theoretical Anchor: Share-of-voice literature (Jones 1990); Ehrenberg-Bass mental availability metrics
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Demand Momentum Signals, Topic Emergence
Example 1: Reading these comments, it's like 8 out of 10 are recommending the same brand.
Example 2: Notice how nobody in this thread is even mentioning the other options?
Example 3: Half the influencers I follow are now talking about this brand specifically.

Construct: Open-ended interest in what a product is or does as a whole, prior to feature-level evaluation. Reflects the early information-gathering phase of the buyer behaviour funnel where the consumer is forming a basic product schema.
Linguistic Surface: Broad, open questions about the product: 'what does this actually do?', 'is this any good?', 'what's the deal with [Product]?', 'curious about this one'.
Boundary: Not Feature Curiosity (which targets a specific named functionality). Not Research Behaviour (which is deeper, technical-spec-level inquiry). Product Curiosity is wide-aperture, low-depth.
Theoretical Anchor: Howard & Sheth (1969) information search; Lavidge-Steiner hierarchy (knowledge stage)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Discovery, Feature Curiosity, Product Exploration
Example 1: What exactly does this product do that others don't?
Example 2: I keep hearing about this — what's the actual point of it?
Example 3: Genuinely curious about what makes this one different.

Construct: Bounded interest in a specific, named functionality of the product. Indicates the consumer has moved past general awareness and is now evaluating the product's attribute composition (Bettman et al. 1998 constructive choice).
Linguistic Surface: Questions or statements naming a particular feature: 'how does the [feature] work?', 'curious about the [feature]', 'is the [feature] any good?'.
Boundary: Not Product Curiosity (whole-product). Not Feature Understanding Questions in Choice Architecture (which signal *confusion* about an existing feature rather than *interest* in learning about it). Feature Curiosity is positive-pull; Feature Understanding Questions are friction-driven.
Theoretical Anchor: Bettman, Luce & Payne (1998) constructive choice; Howard-Sheth attribute search
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Product Curiosity, Research Behaviour, Feature Understanding Questions
Example 1: How does the self-cleaning mode actually work on this?
Example 2: I'm really intrigued by the AI assistant feature — what can it do?
Example 3: Tell me more about the battery — does it really last 2 days?

Construct: Active, self-directed engagement with brand-controlled product information surfaces — websites, demos, product pages, configurators. Indicates the consumer has invested time in independent discovery beyond passive exposure.
Linguistic Surface: Reports of time spent on product surfaces: 'spent an hour on their website', 'been browsing their catalog', 'tried the configurator', 'watched all their demo videos'.
Boundary: Not Research Behaviour (which is technical-document deep-diving). Not Product Curiosity (verbal curiosity without action). Exploration is *behavioural evidence* of investigation.
Theoretical Anchor: Howard-Sheth extended problem-solving phase; Moe (2003) online consumer browsing behaviour
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Research Behaviour, Product Consideration, Feature Curiosity
Example 1: I spent like an hour on their website going through all the models last night.
Example 2: Been playing around with their online configurator — there are so many options.
Example 3: Watched every single demo video they've put out.

Construct: Deep, technical, often cross-source information gathering — whitepapers, specifications, third-party reviews, benchmarks. Indicates high-involvement decision making (Petty & Cacioppo's ELM, 1986) and extensive problem-solving (Howard-Sheth).
Linguistic Surface: References to technical sources or analytical effort: 'reading whitepapers', 'studying the specs', 'going through benchmarks', 'researching for weeks', 'comparing technical documentation'.
Boundary: Not Product Exploration (which is brand-surface engagement). Not Information Seeking in Choice Architecture (which is friction-driven *missing* information). Research Behaviour is sustained, voluntary, and analytical.
Theoretical Anchor: Petty & Cacioppo (1986) ELM central route; Howard-Sheth extended problem-solving
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Product Exploration, Evaluation Initiation, Pros vs Cons Evaluation
Example 1: Been reading the technical whitepapers and benchmark reports for three days straight.
Example 2: I've gone through every review on YouTube and every spec sheet I can find.
Example 3: Did a deep dive into the architecture before deciding to even shortlist this.

Construct: The placement of the brand or product into the consumer's evoked set — the subset of brands actively being weighed for a defined need (Howard & Sheth 1969; Shocker et al. 1991). Indicates qualified, need-anchored interest.
Linguistic Surface: Statements connecting the brand to a specific personal need or use case: 'thinking about this for [purpose]', 'considering this for my [context]', 'might be the right one for [situation]'.
Boundary: Not Shortlisting (which is the narrower final-choice subset). Not Purchase Intent (which is a firm decision). Consideration is open evoked-set membership.
Theoretical Anchor: Howard & Sheth (1969) evoked set; Shocker et al. (1991) consideration set
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Shortlisting Signals, Evaluation Initiation, Pros vs Cons Evaluation
Example 1: I'm seriously thinking about getting this for my home office setup.
Example 2: This is one of the options I'm weighing for the upgrade.
Example 3: Considering it for my daughter — she needs something durable.

Construct: The reduction of the evoked set to a final choice set of typically two to four options — the narrow subset from which the actual decision will be made (Roberts & Lattin 1991 consideration-set models).
Linguistic Surface: Enumeration and elimination language: 'down to', 'between these two', 'final two', 'my last two options', 'narrowed it down to'.
Boundary: Not Product Consideration (broader evoked set). Not Evaluation Initiation (which is the *process* of comparing within the shortlist). Shortlisting is the *state* of having narrowed.
Theoretical Anchor: Roberts & Lattin (1991); Shocker et al. (1991) consideration set
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Evaluation Initiation, Brand Comparison, Decision Confidence Signals
Example 1: It's down to this one and the competitor — final two on my list.
Example 2: I've narrowed it down to three options; this is one of them.
Example 3: Killed the rest of the shortlist; just deciding between these two now.

Construct: The act of beginning a structured comparison process within the shortlist — initiating side-by-side evaluation of attributes, performance, or fit. Marks the transition from exploration to formal deliberation.
Linguistic Surface: Statements about starting comparisons: 'started comparing', 'putting them side by side', 'building a comparison', 'now actually evaluating'.
Boundary: Not Pros vs Cons Evaluation (Choice Architecture — which is the *content* of weighing). Evaluation Initiation is the *kickoff*, before structured pros/cons emerge.
Theoretical Anchor: Bettman, Luce & Payne (1998) constructive choice; Payne (1976) decision strategies
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Shortlisting Signals, Pros vs Cons Evaluation, Trade-off Discussion
Example 1: I've started comparing the specs against my current device.
Example 2: Just opened a tab for each option — time to actually evaluate.
Example 3: Going to spend this weekend doing a proper side-by-side.

Construct: A declared firm commitment to acquire the brand or product. Strongest individual-level predictor of actual purchase in the Fishbein-Ajzen Theory of Reasoned Action (1975) and Ajzen's Theory of Planned Behaviour (1991).
Linguistic Surface: Decisive future-tense or imminent-action language: 'I'm going to buy', 'ordering this', 'about to pull the trigger', 'placing the order', 'decided — buying'.
Boundary: Not Trial Intent (which is reversible/limited). Not First-time Purchase Intent (which adds the no-prior-experience qualifier). Not Repurchase Intent in Reinforcement Stability (which requires active_user state).
Theoretical Anchor: Fishbein & Ajzen (1975) Theory of Reasoned Action; Ajzen (1991) Theory of Planned Behaviour
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: First-time Purchase Intent, Upgrade Intent, Decision Confidence Signals
Example 1: I'm buying the Pro version this weekend. Decided.
Example 2: Placing the order tonight — done deliberating.
Example 3: About to hit the buy button as soon as my paycheck clears.

Construct: A declared desire for a low-commitment, reversible engagement with the product — a sample, demo, trial period, or rental. Indicates the consumer wants to reduce perceived risk before full commitment (Bauer 1960 perceived risk theory).
Linguistic Surface: Requests for reversible engagement: 'is there a trial?', 'can I test it first?', 'free sample?', '7-day return?', 'demo available?', 'try before I buy'.
Boundary: Not Purchase Intent (which is full commitment). Not Competitor Trial Signals (which is trying a rival specifically for comparison). Trial Intent is brand-focused, risk-reducing.
Theoretical Anchor: Bauer (1960) perceived risk; Roselius (1971) risk reduction strategies
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Risk Concerns, Reliability Concerns, First-time Purchase Intent
Example 1: Is there a way to get a sample or a 7-day trial?
Example 2: Do they offer a demo period? I want to try before committing.
Example 3: Anyone know if they do a money-back guarantee? Want to test it first.

Construct: Purchase Intent qualified by the speaker's explicit acknowledgment of no prior experience with the brand. Theoretically important because it indexes *acquisition* rather than *retention* demand — critical for category penetration and growth modelling (Sharp 2010, How Brands Grow).
Linguistic Surface: Purchase Intent language combined with novelty markers: 'never used them before, but', 'first time trying this brand', 'switching from [other] for the first time'.
Boundary: Not Purchase Intent (which is brand-agnostic regarding history). Not Switching Signals (which is comparative and implies abandoning another brand). First-time intent is about the *speaker's* history, not the relational move.
Theoretical Anchor: Sharp (2010) How Brands Grow; Ehrenberg (1959) purchase incidence models
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Purchase Intent, Brand Discovery, Trial Intent
Example 1: I've never bought this brand before, but I'm going to give it a shot this time.
Example 2: First time trying them — fingers crossed.
Example 3: Switching from my usual brand for the first time. Excited and nervous.

Construct: The intent to move within the same brand from a lower tier or older version to a higher tier or newer version. Distinct from cross-brand replacement; indicates within-brand vertical mobility and is a key indicator of brand affinity combined with growth willingness.
Linguistic Surface: Within-brand upgrade language: 'upgrading from my [model]', 'moving up to the Pro', 'getting the Max version', 'going from [old version] to [new version]'.
Boundary: Not Replacement Intent (which can be brand-agnostic or cross-brand). Not Upgrade Loyalty in Reinforcement Stability (which is the *completed* act with loyalty framing). Upgrade Intent is prospective and tier-focused within the brand.
Theoretical Anchor: Ratchford (2001) economics of consumer search; Bayus (1991) product upgrade research
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Upgrade Value Signals, Repurchase Intent, Upgrade Loyalty
Example 1: I have the base model but I'm upgrading to the Max next month.
Example 2: Time to move from my Gen 2 to the new Gen 4.
Example 3: Been on the standard tier for two years — finally ready to go Pro.

Construct: The intent to replace a currently owned product that is failing, ageing, or no longer fit-for-purpose. May or may not specify the replacement brand. Distinct from upgrade because it is driven by *necessity* or *attrition* rather than *aspiration*.
Linguistic Surface: Replacement-need language: 'my current one is dying', 'need to replace', 'time for a new one', 'this one broke', 'it's the end of life for my [product]'.
Boundary: Not Upgrade Intent (which is aspirational, within-brand, tier-focused). Replacement Intent often creates a *new* shortlisting moment and can fire alongside Switching Signals if the speaker is considering a different brand.
Theoretical Anchor: Bayus (1991) product replacement; Antonides (1991) durable replacement decisions
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Switching Signals, Brand Comparison, Purchase Intent
Example 1: My laptop is on its last legs — I need to replace it ASAP.
Example 2: After 6 years my old one finally gave up. Time for a new one.
Example 3: The screen cracked beyond repair — getting a replacement this weekend.

Construct: Consumer awareness of where a specific product sits within its market lifecycle — introduction, growth, maturity, or decline (Levitt 1965 product lifecycle theory). Influences timing decisions around purchase, upgrade, and inventory.
Linguistic Surface: Lifecycle-stage queries and observations: 'is this still being made?', 'is a new version coming?', 'is the current model end-of-life?', 'should I wait for the refresh?'.
Boundary: Not Product Launch Interest (Market Tempo — which is forward-looking anticipation of a *new* launch). Lifecycle Signals are about the *current* product's position in time, often relative to a potential successor.
Theoretical Anchor: Levitt (1965) product lifecycle theory; Day (1981) PLC empirical evidence
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Purchase Delay Signals, Discount Expectation, Product Launch Interest
Example 1: Is a new version coming out soon, or is this the latest model?
Example 2: Is this end-of-life? Don't want to buy something they're about to discontinue.
Example 3: When's the next refresh expected? Worth waiting or buying now?

Construct: The subjective experience of cognitive and emotional fatigue arising from having too many options to evaluate. Empirically established in Iyengar & Lepper (2000) as reducing both decision satisfaction and likelihood of purchase. Reflects an input-level failure of the choice architecture.
Linguistic Surface: Fatigue and overwhelm language tied to option counts: 'too many choices', 'overwhelmed', 'so many options I can't keep track', 'my head is spinning', 'don't know where to start'.
Boundary: Not Decision Paralysis (which is the *behavioural* outcome of overload — deferral or non-choice). Overload describes the *experience* of input excess; paralysis describes the *output*.
Theoretical Anchor: Iyengar & Lepper (2000) jam study; Schwartz (2004) Paradox of Choice; Scheibehenne et al. (2010) meta-analysis
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Decision Paralysis, Option Abundance Language, Advice Seeking Signals
Example 1: I'm looking at 12 different models and I genuinely have no idea which is for me.
Example 2: There are just way too many variants — my head is spinning.
Example 3: Honestly overwhelmed by the number of options. Where do I even start?

Construct: The behavioural outcome of choice difficulty — explicit deferral, abandonment, or non-decision. Represents the consumer's exit from the funnel due to friction rather than disinterest. Strongly predictive of lost conversion.
Linguistic Surface: Statements of abandonment or stalled action: 'can't decide', 'might just not buy anything', 'giving up', 'putting it on hold indefinitely', 'too hard, walking away'.
Boundary: Not Choice Overload (the *cause*). Not Purchase Delay Signals (which give a *reason* for delay — waiting for a launch, sale, etc.). Paralysis has no exit plan.
Theoretical Anchor: Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz (2006) deferred decision-making; Anderson (2003) decision avoidance
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Choice Overload, Decision Uncertainty, Risk Concerns
Example 1: I've been looking for weeks and still can't decide. I might just not buy anything.
Example 2: Honestly given up trying to pick one. Sticking with what I have.
Example 3: Too many trade-offs — I keep going in circles. Just walking away.

Construct: Descriptive observations about the breadth of options available in the category — neutral or evaluative commentary on assortment size without necessarily expressing personal overload.
Linguistic Surface: Observational statements about assortment: 'huge catalog', 'they have everything', 'so many variants', 'massive selection', 'options for every use case'.
Boundary: Not Choice Overload (which is the *fatigue response*). Option Abundance can be positive ('great variety') or simply observational. This signal becomes Choice Overload only when paired with fatigue language.
Theoretical Anchor: Iyengar & Lepper (2000) assortment effects; Chernev (2003) assortment perception
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Choice Overload, Product Exploration
Example 1: Their catalog is huge — they have a model for literally every use case.
Example 2: The variety is unreal. There's something for everyone.
Example 3: So many SKUs in this category right now, it's wild.

Construct: Active requests for specific, missing factual information about a product or feature. Indicates an information gap in the brand's communication that is blocking the decision process — a *data gap*.
Linguistic Surface: Direct factual questions about absent information: 'does it have [X]?', 'what is the [spec]?', 'is it [property]?', 'does anyone know if [factual question]?'.
Boundary: Not Clarification Requests (which target *ambiguous existing* information — a semantic gap). Not Feature Understanding Questions (which target *how* a feature works — a mental-model gap). Information Seeking targets pure missing facts.
Theoretical Anchor: Belkin (1980) Anomalous States of Knowledge; Wilson (1999) information-seeking behaviour
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Clarification Requests, Feature Understanding Questions, Research Behaviour
Example 1: Does anyone know the actual dimensions of the charging port?
Example 2: Can't find this anywhere — is it waterproof or just splash-resistant?
Example 3: What's the warranty period? The website doesn't say.

Construct: Requests for simpler or clearer explanations of information that *is* available but is ambiguous, jargon-heavy, or poorly presented. Indicates a *semantic gap* in the brand's communication.
Linguistic Surface: Requests for re-explanation or simplification: 'can someone explain', 'in plain English', 'what does this actually mean?', 'translate this for me', 'ELI5'.
Boundary: Not Information Seeking (which requests *missing* facts). Not Feature Understanding Questions (which target the *mechanism* of how a feature works). Clarification is about *legibility* of existing info.
Theoretical Anchor: Belkin (1980) information needs; Grice's cooperative principle (1975) on clarity
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Information Seeking, Feature Understanding Questions
Example 1: Can someone explain the difference between these two tiers in plain English?
Example 2: What does 'adaptive sync rendering' even mean? ELI5.
Example 3: Their spec sheet is gibberish — anyone want to translate?

Construct: Questions targeting the underlying mechanism, logic, or use-context of a named feature. Indicates a *mental-model gap* — the consumer knows the feature exists and what it's called but cannot predict how it behaves.
Linguistic Surface: Mechanism questions: 'how does [feature] work?', 'when does [feature] kick in?', 'what triggers [feature]?', 'how is [feature A] different from [feature B]?'.
Boundary: Not Information Seeking (missing fact). Not Clarification Requests (ambiguous info). Not Feature Curiosity (Demand Gravity — which is *interest* in learning, not confusion). Understanding Questions assume awareness and seek mechanism.
Theoretical Anchor: Norman (1988) mental models; Johnson-Laird (1983) mental models theory
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Curiosity, Clarification Requests, Information Seeking
Example 1: I don't get how 'Smart Sensing' is actually different from a normal motion sensor.
Example 2: When does the eco mode kick in? Is it automatic or do I toggle it?
Example 3: Confused about how the AI assistant decides which suggestion to give.

Construct: Specific requests for personalised suggestions based on stated needs, constraints, or context. Indicates the consumer has externalised the choice problem and is seeking expert or peer input to scaffold the decision.
Linguistic Surface: Need-anchored requests: 'what's the best for [use case]?', 'I'm a [persona] — what should I get?', 'recommendations for [constraint]?'.
Boundary: Not Peer Advice Seeking (which targets user *experience* rather than recommendations). Not Community Consultation (which is validation of an already-formed choice). Recommendation Requests are open-ended need-to-product mapping.
Theoretical Anchor: Bansal & Voyer (2000) word-of-mouth and search; Murray (1991) personal influence
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Peer Advice Seeking, Community Consultation, Choice Overload
Example 1: I'm a student on a tight budget — what's the best laptop for me?
Example 2: Recommendations for someone who travels a lot and needs long battery life?
Example 3: What should I get if I mostly use it for photo editing?

Construct: Requests directed at existing users for their first-hand experience with a specific product or feature. Distinguished by its targeted, experiential nature — the consumer wants lived data, not recommendations.
Linguistic Surface: Owner-targeted questions: 'anyone who owns this — does it actually [X]?', 'real users, is the [feature] worth it?', 'people who've had this for a year, how is it holding up?'.
Boundary: Not Recommendation Requests (which ask for suggestions, not experience reports). Not Community Consultation (which seeks validation of a specific intended choice).
Theoretical Anchor: Murray (1991) personal information sources; Brown & Reingen (1987) word-of-mouth tie strength
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Recommendation Requests, Community Consultation, Reliability Concerns
Example 1: To anyone who owns this — do you actually use the secondary screen?
Example 2: Owners only please: how is the battery after 6 months of use?
Example 3: Real users — is the noise as bad as the reviews say?

Construct: Using a forum, group, or community to validate a tentative choice already formed. The consumer has done the work; they are seeking social confirmation before committing. Functions as a risk-reduction mechanism.
Linguistic Surface: Validation-seeking statements: 'is this a good choice?', 'thinking of getting this — thoughts?', 'am I making a mistake?', 'about to buy — anyone want to talk me out of it?'.
Boundary: Not Recommendation Requests (which is open-ended). Not Peer Advice Seeking (which is experiential data-gathering). Community Consultation is choice-validation.
Theoretical Anchor: Cialdini (1984) social proof; Festinger (1954) social comparison theory
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Decision Confidence Signals, Decision Uncertainty, Advice Seeking Signals
Example 1: About to pull the trigger on this — anyone want to talk me out of it?
Example 2: Posting here for a sanity check: is this a good deal at this price?
Example 3: I think this is the right pick but want the community's take before I buy.

Construct: Explicit expression of doubt or hesitation about a tentative or near-final choice. Reflects incomplete confidence and unresolved trade-offs. Predictive of conversion drop-off if unresolved.
Linguistic Surface: Hedging and doubt language: 'still hesitant', 'not 100% sure', 'something's holding me back', '90% there but', 'gut says no'.
Boundary: Not Decision Paralysis (which is full stall). Not Risk Concerns (which name a specific fear). Uncertainty is unresolved residual doubt without a specific named cause.
Theoretical Anchor: Bettman et al. (1998) decision confidence; Tversky & Shafir (1992) decision conflict
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Decision Paralysis, Risk Concerns, Community Consultation
Example 1: I'm 90% sure about this one but that 10% is keeping me from buying.
Example 2: Something feels off — can't quite put my finger on it.
Example 3: Gut says yes but my head keeps saying wait.

Construct: Prospective, probabilistic fears about negative outcomes of a purchase — financial, functional, social, psychological, or temporal. Anchored in Bauer's (1960) perceived risk theory and Jacoby & Kaplan's (1972) typology of risk dimensions.
Linguistic Surface: Hypothetical worry framing: 'what if it breaks?', 'worried it'll [bad outcome]', 'concerned about [risk]', 'is this safe to buy from them?'.
Boundary: Not Reliability Concerns (which is *evidence-based* doubt based on observed failure patterns). Risk Concerns are prospective and probabilistic — the bad thing has not yet happened.
Theoretical Anchor: Bauer (1960) perceived risk; Jacoby & Kaplan (1972) risk dimensions; Roselius (1971)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Reliability Concerns, Decision Uncertainty, Trial Intent
Example 1: What if it breaks after a month? Is the warranty actually good?
Example 2: Worried about buying from a brand I don't know — what if it's a dud?
Example 3: What if I hate it after a week? Returns process is unclear.

Construct: Doubt about the long-term performance of the product, grounded in observed evidence — reviews, reports, peer experience, or first-hand patterns. Differs from Risk Concerns by being evidence-anchored rather than speculative.
Linguistic Surface: Evidence-cited doubt: 'I've heard these have [issue]', 'too many reports of [failure]', 'my friend's broke after [time]', 'reviews mention [problem] a lot'.
Boundary: Not Risk Concerns (hypothetical). Not Competitor Reliability Issues (which target a rival brand's reliability). Reliability Concerns are observed-evidence doubts about *this* brand.
Theoretical Anchor: Mitchell (1999) consumer perceived risk re-examined; Roselius (1971) risk reduction
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Risk Concerns, Competitor Reliability Issues, Decision Uncertainty
Example 1: I've heard these have battery issues after about a year of use.
Example 2: Too many reports of the hinge breaking — that's a red flag for me.
Example 3: My friend's unit died after 8 months. Worried this is a pattern.

Construct: Explicit listing or weighing of positives and negatives of an option. Reflects the additive compensatory decision strategy (Weighted Additive Rule — Bettman et al. 1998) where attributes are summed across favourable and unfavourable dimensions.
Linguistic Surface: Pro/con enumerative phrasing: 'on the plus side... on the minus...', '[positive] but [negative]', 'pros are X, cons are Y', 'love the [feature] but hate the [feature]'.
Boundary: Not Trade-off Discussion (which is about *sacrificing* one attribute for another — a lexicographic or non-compensatory move). Pros vs Cons treats attributes as additive; Trade-off is about substitution.
Theoretical Anchor: Bettman, Luce & Payne (1998) constructive choice; Payne (1976) decision strategies
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Trade-off Discussion, Feature Importance Discussion, Evaluation Initiation
Example 1: Screen is great, but the battery life is a huge con for me.
Example 2: Pros: build quality, software. Cons: price, weight. Still deciding.
Example 3: I love the design but the lack of ports is killing me.

Construct: Explicit reasoning about what attribute must be sacrificed to gain another — non-compensatory decision logic where the consumer accepts a downside in exchange for a prioritised upside.
Linguistic Surface: Sacrifice-for-gain phrasing: 'okay with X if Y', 'willing to give up X for Y', 'trading [worse attribute] for [better attribute]'.
Boundary: Not Pros vs Cons (additive). Not Feature Importance Discussion (which is *ranking* attributes without naming a trade). Trade-off Discussion explicitly accepts a sacrifice.
Theoretical Anchor: Tversky (1972) elimination by aspects; Payne (1976) decision strategies; Bettman et al. (1998)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Pros vs Cons Evaluation, Feature Importance Discussion, Value Evaluation Signals
Example 1: I'm fine paying more if it means better customer support.
Example 2: Willing to give up some battery life for a thinner design.
Example 3: Happy to sacrifice portability for raw performance in this case.

Construct: Explicit ranking or weighting of which product attributes matter most to the consumer's decision. Reveals individual preference structure and attribute salience hierarchies (Bettman et al. 1998).
Linguistic Surface: Priority language: 'X matters more than Y', 'most important to me is [feature]', '[feature] is non-negotiable', 'I don't care about [feature]'.
Boundary: Not Trade-off Discussion (which names a specific sacrifice). Not Pros vs Cons (which evaluates additively). Feature Importance Discussion sets the *weights* before evaluation.
Theoretical Anchor: Bettman, Luce & Payne (1998); Wilkie & Pessemier (1973) multi-attribute models
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Pros vs Cons Evaluation, Trade-off Discussion, Evaluation Initiation
Example 1: Honestly the camera doesn't matter much to me — storage is the priority.
Example 2: For me battery life is non-negotiable; everything else is secondary.
Example 3: Build quality matters most. Specs come second.

Construct: Deferral of a purchase decision with an *explicit, reasoned cause* — waiting for a price drop, upcoming launch, financial readiness, or competitor announcement. Diagnostic because it reveals the specific blocker keeping conversion suspended.
Linguistic Surface: Delay-with-reason language: 'holding off until [event]', 'waiting for [trigger]', 'going to wait and see if [condition]'.
Boundary: Not Decision Timing Signals (neutral scheduling without specific reason). Not Decision Paralysis (which has no resolution plan). Purchase Delay has both intent and a named condition.
Theoretical Anchor: Greenleaf & Lehmann (1995) reasons for purchase delay; Anderson (2003) choice deferral
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Discount Expectation, Product Launch Interest, Decision Timing Signals
Example 1: Holding off until I see what their competitor launches next month.
Example 2: Waiting for Black Friday — no way I'm paying full price.
Example 3: Going to wait until the Gen 4 drops before deciding.

Construct: Neutral statements about *when* a decision will be made, without a specific blocking cause. Indicates the consumer has a temporal frame for the decision but no urgency or external trigger.
Linguistic Surface: Temporal placeholders: 'planning to decide next month', 'end of quarter', 'sometime soon', 'no rush'.
Boundary: Not Purchase Delay (which carries an explicit reason for delay). Timing Signals are scheduling rather than blocking.
Theoretical Anchor: Greenleaf & Lehmann (1995); Bettman et al. (1998) decision context
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Purchase Delay Signals, Research Stage Signals
Example 1: Planning to decide by the end of the month.
Example 2: No rush — sometime in the next quarter probably.
Example 3: I'll make up my mind once I get back from my trip.

Construct: Self-reported indicators of where the consumer is in the funnel — early-stage browsing, mid-stage shortlisting, late-stage validation. Useful for funnel-stage attribution of subsequent signals.
Linguistic Surface: Funnel-position language: 'just starting to look', 'early stages', 'still doing research', 'almost done deciding', 'late-stage check'.
Boundary: Not Research Behaviour (Demand Gravity — which is the *activity* of research). Research Stage Signals are *self-localisation* in the funnel.
Theoretical Anchor: Howard-Sheth (1969) funnel stages; Court et al. (2009) Consumer Decision Journey
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Research Behaviour, Decision Timing Signals, Product Curiosity
Example 1: Still in the very early stages of looking into this category.
Example 2: Almost done with my research — final lap.
Example 3: Just starting to explore. Not buying for a while.

Construct: The consumer's subjective evaluation of where a price *feels* on a perceived scale — cheap, fair, premium, expensive, exorbitant — independent of explicit comparison. Reflects the internal reference price formed through category exposure (Monroe 1973; Winer 1986).
Linguistic Surface: Descriptive characterisations of the price level: 'feels premium', 'seems reasonable', 'looks expensive', 'priced well', 'feels overpriced'.
Boundary: Not Price Sensitivity (which is reactive to change). Not Value for Money (which is benefit-anchored judgment). Price Perception is the raw *feel* of the price tag in isolation.
Theoretical Anchor: Monroe (1973) reference price; Winer (1986) reference price models; Kahneman & Tversky (1979)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Value for Money, Price Sensitivity, Price Acceptance
Example 1: For what it offers, the price seems quite reasonable.
Example 2: Honestly it feels like a premium price for a premium product.
Example 3: The price tag feels steep for what is essentially a basic model.

Construct: Conditional reactivity to small price changes or price thresholds — the consumer indicates their demand would change materially with modest price movement. Operationalises elasticity at the individual level (Tellis 1988 meta-analysis on price elasticity).
Linguistic Surface: Conditional change language: 'if it goes up by [X], I'm out', 'at this price yes, at $50 more no', 'price-sensitive shopper here', 'any more expensive and I walk'.
Boundary: Not Price Shock (acute affective reaction). Not Price Resistance (active refusal at current level). Price Sensitivity is conditional and threshold-anchored.
Theoretical Anchor: Tellis (1988) price elasticity meta-analysis; Goldsmith et al. (2010) price sensitivity scale
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Price Shock, Price Resistance, Discount Expectation
Example 1: If the price goes up even by 5%, I'm out.
Example 2: At this price yes, but a hundred bucks more and I'm walking.
Example 3: Very price-sensitive in this category — every dollar matters.

Construct: Acute affective response to an unexpected price level — surprise, outrage, or sticker shock. Indicates a strong gap between expected and observed price, often disqualifying the brand from further consideration in the moment.
Linguistic Surface: Exclamatory and reactive language: 'WHAT?!', 'are they serious?', 'no way that costs that much', 'how much?!', 'that is insane'.
Boundary: Not Price Sensitivity (conditional). Not Price Resistance (which is reasoned refusal). Price Shock is the *initial emotional reaction*; resistance often follows after shock subsides.
Theoretical Anchor: Kahneman & Tversky (1979) Prospect Theory; Thaler (1985) transaction utility
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Price Resistance, Price Sensitivity, Competitor Value Criticism
Example 1: I was interested until I saw the price tag. That is INSANE.
Example 2: Wait, HOW much? You've got to be joking.
Example 3: Holy hell that's expensive. Did not expect that number.

Construct: Reasoned refusal to buy at the current price level despite favourable evaluation of the product. The consumer values the product but the price has crossed their willingness-to-pay threshold.
Linguistic Surface: Justification-paired refusal: 'love it but can't justify', 'great product, wrong price', 'won't pay that much for it', 'as good as it is, no'.
Boundary: Not Price Shock (emotional). Not Price Sensitivity (conditional). Price Resistance is a *decision* made — refusal at the current level.
Theoretical Anchor: Adaval & Monroe (2002) reservation price; Wertenbroch & Skiera (2002) willingness-to-pay
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Price Sensitivity, Discount Expectation, Value Evaluation Signals
Example 1: I love it, but I simply cannot justify spending that much money.
Example 2: Beautiful product. Won't be paying that for it though.
Example 3: It's great but I refuse to pay over a thousand for this.

Construct: Reasoned acquiescence to a high price, often framed as 'you get what you pay for'. Indicates low elasticity for this consumer — they have rationalised the cost into their value frame.
Linguistic Surface: Acceptance with justification: 'expensive but worth it', 'pricey for a reason', 'that's the cost of [quality]', 'I knew what I was signing up for'.
Boundary: Not Value for Money (which is broader judgment of total worth). Price Acceptance specifically addresses *the price as given* and accepts it.
Theoretical Anchor: Rao & Monroe (1989) price-quality inference; Erickson & Johansson (1985)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Value for Money, Cost Justification, Price Perception
Example 1: It's expensive, but that's what you pay for this kind of quality.
Example 2: Yeah it's pricey, but you get what you pay for. Worth it.
Example 3: Knew it would cost a lot going in. Fine with that.

Construct: Holistic, often gut-level assessment of whether the product's total benefit justifies its total cost. The most general value judgment, integrating functional, emotional, and symbolic worth (Zeithaml 1988 perceived value).
Linguistic Surface: Total-value judgments: 'great value', 'best bang for buck', 'worth every penny', 'rip-off', 'good value', 'overpriced for what it is'.
Boundary: Not Cost-Benefit Signals (which involve explicit ledger reasoning). Not ROI Perception (which frames as investment with future return). Value for Money is holistic gut judgment.
Theoretical Anchor: Zeithaml (1988) perceived value; Sweeney & Soutar (2001) PERVAL scale
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Cost-Benefit Signals, Price Acceptance, Feature Value Perception
Example 1: Easily the best value for money in the market right now.
Example 2: Honestly it's overpriced for what you actually get.
Example 3: Worth every rupee. No regrets.

Construct: The consumer's assessment of the worth of a *specific feature or component* relative to its contribution to the price. Useful for identifying which features carry the premium and which are perceived as filler.
Linguistic Surface: Feature-specific worth statements: 'the [feature] alone is worth it', 'paying extra just for [feature]', '[feature] is the only reason this is worth the price'.
Boundary: Not Value for Money (holistic). Not Feature Superiority Claims (Competitive Energy — which is *comparative* against rivals). Feature Value Perception is brand-internal price attribution.
Theoretical Anchor: Bettman et al. (1998) attribute valuation; Hauser & Urban (1986) value priority
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Value for Money, Innovation Value, Performance Value
Example 1: The AI features alone are why I'd pay this much.
Example 2: Honestly the keyboard alone justifies the price.
Example 3: Half of these features I don't use — they're padding the price.

Construct: Worth assigned specifically to *novel or unique* features that have no equivalent elsewhere in the market. Indicates willingness to pay a premium for innovation, a key driver of early-adopter behaviour (Rogers 1962).
Linguistic Surface: Uniqueness-anchored value language: 'no one else does this', 'first of its kind', 'paying more because it's unique', 'cutting-edge', 'haven't seen this anywhere else'.
Boundary: Not Feature Value Perception (which can be for any feature, novel or not). Not Performance Value (which is anchored in benchmarks, not novelty). Innovation Value is uniqueness-driven.
Theoretical Anchor: Rogers (1962) Diffusion of Innovations; Holak & Lehmann (1990) innovation attributes
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Value Perception, Product Launch Interest, Performance Value
Example 1: Willing to pay more because no one else has this tech.
Example 2: It's a first-of-its-kind feature — that's worth the premium.
Example 3: This is the only brand offering this. Easy to justify the price.

Construct: Value assigned to measurable technical or performance benchmarks — speed, durability, capacity, efficiency. Reflects utilitarian dimension of perceived value (Sweeney & Soutar 2001).
Linguistic Surface: Benchmark-anchored worth: 'the speed alone is worth it', 'pays for itself in performance', 'numbers don't lie — worth the cost'.
Boundary: Not Innovation Value (uniqueness-driven). Not Performance Superiority (Competitive Energy — *comparative*). Performance Value is utility-per-cost.
Theoretical Anchor: Sweeney & Soutar (2001) PERVAL functional value; Holbrook (1999) consumer value
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Value Perception, Value for Money, ROI Perception
Example 1: The speed alone makes it worth every penny.
Example 2: For raw performance per dollar, nothing comes close.
Example 3: Benchmarks justify the price. End of debate.

Construct: User-provided rationalisations for spending the money — explicit reasons the high cost is acceptable. Indicates active mental accounting and reduction of post-purchase cognitive dissonance (Festinger 1957).
Linguistic Surface: Reasoning-out-loud language: 'an investment in [domain]', 'tax-deductible', 'will pay for itself', 'cheaper than [alternative]', 'spread over [time]'.
Boundary: Not Price Acceptance (general acquiescence). Not ROI Perception (which specifically frames as investment). Cost Justification provides explicit reasoning to overcome price resistance.
Theoretical Anchor: Festinger (1957) cognitive dissonance; Thaler (1985) mental accounting
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: ROI Perception, Price Acceptance, Cost-Benefit Signals
Example 1: It's an investment in my career, so the price is fine.
Example 2: Spread over 3 years of use, it's actually quite cheap per day.
Example 3: I'll save more on coffee shop wifi by working from home — it pays for itself.

Construct: Framing of the purchase as an investment with quantifiable or temporal return. The consumer explicitly calculates payback period, savings generated, or revenue/productivity unlocked.
Linguistic Surface: Return-framing: 'will pay for itself in [time]', 'saves me [amount/time] per [period]', 'recoup the cost in [duration]'.
Boundary: Not Cost Justification (broader rationalisation). Not Performance Value (which is utility-anchored without explicit return calculus). ROI Perception requires a return frame.
Theoretical Anchor: Kahneman & Tversky (1984) prospect theory framing; Thaler (1985) transaction utility
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Cost Justification, Performance Value, Cost-Benefit Signals
Example 1: This machine will pay for itself within three months of use.
Example 2: Saves me 2 hours a day — easy to see the return.
Example 3: ROI on this is under a year if I keep using it daily.

Construct: Explicit ledger-style reasoning weighing specific costs against specific benefits. Indicates deliberate analytical processing (Petty & Cacioppo's ELM central route, 1986).
Linguistic Surface: Ledger-language: 'is the extra [cost] worth the [benefit]?', 'doing the maths', '[cost] in exchange for [benefit]'.
Boundary: Not Value for Money (holistic gut). Not Trade-off Discussion (Choice Architecture — attribute substitution). Cost-Benefit Signals are explicit price-vs-benefit accounting.
Theoretical Anchor: Petty & Cacioppo (1986) ELM; Bettman et al. (1998) constructive choice
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Value Evaluation Signals, Cost Justification, Trade-off Discussion
Example 1: Is the extra $200 actually worth the slightly better screen?
Example 2: Doing the maths on whether the Pro version pays back the difference.
Example 3: Sitting with a spreadsheet weighing the upgrade cost vs benefit.

Construct: Awareness and articulation of long-term or hidden costs of ownership — maintenance, accessories, subscriptions, repairs, energy. Reflects total-cost-of-ownership thinking, often a sign of experienced or analytical buyers.
Linguistic Surface: Recurring-cost language: 'cost of ownership', 'filters add up', 'subscription on top', 'maintenance is expensive', 'hidden costs'.
Boundary: Not Price Perception (initial price). Not Cost-Benefit Signals (one-time accounting). Ownership Cost Discussion is specifically about ongoing or hidden cost streams.
Theoretical Anchor: Ferrin & Plank (2002) total cost of ownership; Ellram (1995) TCO models
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Cost-Benefit Signals, Value for Money, Reliability Concerns
Example 1: The filters and maintenance add up to quite a bit over the years.
Example 2: Sticker price is fine but the subscription on top is another story.
Example 3: Total cost of ownership is way higher than the upfront cost suggests.

Construct: Assumption or anticipation that a sale, promotion, or discount will arrive — leading to deferred purchase. Indicates the consumer has internalised the brand's or category's promotion cadence (Krishna 1992 reference-price effects of promotions).
Linguistic Surface: Sale-anticipation language: 'waiting for Black Friday', 'they always discount in [period]', 'never buy at full price', 'sale must be coming'.
Boundary: Not Deal Sensitivity (which is response to *available* offers). Discount Expectation is prospective and condition-creating.
Theoretical Anchor: Krishna (1992) reference-price effects of promotions; Mela et al. (1997) long-term promo effects
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Purchase Delay Signals, Deal Sensitivity, Price Sensitivity
Example 1: Waiting for the Black Friday deal before I commit.
Example 2: They always discount during the festive season — I'll hold.
Example 3: Never buy this brand at full price. Sale is coming.

Construct: Active response to currently available promotions, bundles, or limited offers. Indicates the promotion is materially shifting the perceived value equation.
Linguistic Surface: Reactive deal language: 'this offer makes it a great deal', 'free accessories included', 'won't get this price again', 'pulling the trigger because of the sale'.
Boundary: Not Discount Expectation (anticipatory). Not Bundle Value Perception (which is specifically about bundle worth). Deal Sensitivity is reactive to a *current* offer.
Theoretical Anchor: Blattberg, Briesch & Fox (1995) promotions research; Chandon, Wansink & Laurent (2000)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Bundle Value Perception, Discount Expectation, Purchase Intent
Example 1: The free accessories make this a much better deal than the standalone.
Example 2: 20% off plus free shipping — buying now.
Example 3: This offer won't come around again. Pulling the trigger.

Construct: Specific evaluation of the worth of a multi-item or package deal compared to its components. Reflects mental accounting (Thaler 1985) and the perceived premium of integrated offerings.
Linguistic Surface: Bundle-vs-piecemeal language: 'cheaper than buying separately', 'bundle is the way to go', 'set is better value', 'why would I buy them individually?'.
Boundary: Not Deal Sensitivity (broader response to offers). Not Value for Money (holistic single-product). Bundle Value Perception is multi-item-versus-aggregate.
Theoretical Anchor: Thaler (1985) mental accounting; Stremersch & Tellis (2002) product bundling
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Deal Sensitivity, Value for Money, Cost-Benefit Signals
Example 1: Buying the set is way cheaper than buying them individually.
Example 2: The bundle is honestly the smartest way to get into this ecosystem.
Example 3: Bundle saves about 30% over à la carte — easy choice.

Construct: Specific evaluation of whether moving from version N to version N+1 is justified by the improvement delta. Distinct from cross-brand evaluation; this is brand-internal generational comparison.
Linguistic Surface: Generational-jump language: 'is the Gen 3 really that much better than Gen 2?', 'worth upgrading from [old]?', 'enough improvement to justify the upgrade?'.
Boundary: Not Upgrade Intent (Demand Gravity — which is the *decision* to upgrade). Upgrade Worthiness is the *evaluation* of whether to upgrade. Not Incremental Value Perception (broader).
Theoretical Anchor: Bayus (1991) product replacement; Okada (2001) trade-in research
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Upgrade Intent, Incremental Value Perception, Cost-Benefit Signals
Example 1: Is the Gen 4 really that much better than my Gen 3?
Example 2: My current one works fine — don't see enough reason to upgrade yet.
Example 3: Just three minor changes between versions. Not worth the upgrade money.

Construct: Evaluation of whether *small improvements* — across versions, tiers, or brands — justify their marginal cost. Captures diminishing returns sensitivity at the upper tail of the product line.
Linguistic Surface: Marginal-improvement language: 'the minor speed boost isn't worth $100', 'tiny improvement, huge price jump', 'diminishing returns'.
Boundary: Not Upgrade Worthiness (which is specifically generational). Incremental Value Perception applies broadly to tiers and small jumps within or across product lines.
Theoretical Anchor: Kahneman & Tversky (1979) Prospect Theory diminishing sensitivity; Thaler (1985)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Upgrade Worthiness, Cost-Benefit Signals, Value for Money
Example 1: The minor speed bump isn't worth an extra $100.
Example 2: Diminishing returns at this tier — the jump from Pro to Max is just not worth it.
Example 3: Tiny incremental gains for a massive price increase. Pass.

Construct: Post-purchase confirmation that the product met expectations. The base layer of Oliver's four-stage loyalty hierarchy (1980 expectancy-disconfirmation model). Necessary but not sufficient for higher loyalty layers.
Linguistic Surface: Confirmation language: 'happy with', 'satisfied', 'no complaints', 'works as advertised', 'does what it should'.
Boundary: Not Delight Signals (which require *unexpected* positive surprise). Not Positive Experience Narratives (longer-form story). Experience Satisfaction is steady-state confirmation.
Theoretical Anchor: Oliver (1980) expectancy-disconfirmation; Oliver (1999) four-stage loyalty
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Delight Signals, Product Reliability, Repurchase Intent
Example 1: Been using it for a month and I'm very happy with it.
Example 2: Honestly no complaints — does what it's supposed to do.
Example 3: Solid product. Lived up to expectations.

Construct: Affective response to *unexpected* positive aspects of the experience that exceeded expectations. A stronger predictor of advocacy than satisfaction alone (Rust & Oliver 2000).
Linguistic Surface: Surprise markers + positive evaluation: 'didn't expect', 'pleasantly surprised', 'wow', 'blown away', 'far better than I thought', 'didn't think it would [positive outcome]'.
Boundary: Not Experience Satisfaction (meets expectations). Not Positive Experience Narratives (which is the long-form story). Delight requires the *surprise* element.
Theoretical Anchor: Rust & Oliver (2000) delight vs satisfaction; Plutchik (1980) surprise as primary emotion
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Experience Satisfaction, Positive Experience Narratives, Advocacy Signals
Example 1: I didn't expect it to be this quiet — what a pleasant surprise.
Example 2: Wow. Way better than I thought it would be.
Example 3: Honestly blown away. Did not see that level of build quality coming.

Construct: Detailed, story-form recounting of a positive product experience — typically multi-sentence with context, action, and outcome. Indicates strong memory encoding of the brand experience (Schank & Abelson 1995 narrative memory).
Linguistic Surface: Story structure with setup-action-outcome: 'let me tell you about', 'so the other day', 'long story short', extended past-tense narration.
Boundary: Not Experience Satisfaction (which is a *short* confirmation). Not Delight Signals (which can be brief). Narratives are extended and structured.
Theoretical Anchor: Schank & Abelson (1995) narrative memory; Escalas (2004) brand narrative transportation
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Delight Signals, Advocacy Signals, Word-of-mouth Promotion
Example 1: Let me tell you how this thing saved my project last night — I was up at 2am with a deadline...
Example 2: So I took it on a 3-week trip across India. Dropped twice, drenched once. Still works perfectly.
Example 3: Long story short — power went out, this kept my home running for six hours. Worth every rupee.

Construct: Reports of consistent, durable performance over time — evidence of the product holding up under real-world conditions. Foundational driver of trust and behavioural loyalty (Garbarino & Johnson 1999).
Linguistic Surface: Durability and consistency reports: 'still works after [time]', 'never failed me', 'dropped it twice and still fine', 'rock solid', 'no issues in [duration]'.
Boundary: Not Long-term Ownership Narratives (which is multi-year story-form). Not Experience Satisfaction (which is general happiness). Product Reliability is specifically about consistent function.
Theoretical Anchor: Garbarino & Johnson (1999) trust and loyalty; Selnes (1993) service quality and loyalty
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Experience Satisfaction, Long-term Ownership Narratives, Brand Defense
Example 1: Dropped it twice and it still works perfectly.
Example 2: Three years in and not a single issue.
Example 3: Rock solid — has never crashed or failed on me once.

Construct: Stated plan to buy from the same brand again at the next purchase occasion. Operationalises conative loyalty in Oliver's (1999) framework — commitment that has not yet translated into action.
Linguistic Surface: Future-tense same-brand language: 'will buy this brand again', 'sticking with them next time', 'when this one dies, getting another'.
Boundary: Not Upgrade Loyalty (which is *upgrading* within brand, often action-completed). Not Purchase Intent (Demand Gravity — first-time or non-loyalty-anchored). Repurchase Intent is loyalty-anchored and prospective.
Theoretical Anchor: Oliver (1999) conative loyalty; Dick & Basu (1994) loyalty matrix
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Upgrade Loyalty, Brand Advocacy, Experience Satisfaction
Example 1: When this one finally dies, I'm definitely buying the same brand again.
Example 2: Sticking with them for my next phone for sure.
Example 3: Already planning my next purchase from them. Loyal customer.

Construct: Completed or imminent within-brand upgrade behaviour framed in loyalty terms — the consumer explicitly notes they are *staying with the brand* while moving up. Represents action loyalty (Oliver 1999 final stage).
Linguistic Surface: Upgrade + loyalty markers: 'traded my old [Brand] for the new [Brand]', 'always a [Brand] fan', 'stayed within the family', 'X generations deep with this brand'.
Boundary: Not Upgrade Intent (Demand Gravity — prospective, not loyalty-framed). Not Repurchase Intent (future, brand-only). Upgrade Loyalty is completed within-brand action with loyalty self-identification.
Theoretical Anchor: Oliver (1999) action loyalty; Bayus (1991) upgrade behaviour
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Repurchase Intent, Brand Advocacy, Identity Signals
Example 1: Just traded in my old model for the new one. Always a fan.
Example 2: Five generations deep with this brand. Why switch?
Example 3: Upgraded within the family again. Wouldn't dream of going elsewhere.

Construct: Indicators of non-deliberative, automatic, repeated use of the product without conscious decision-making each time. Operationalises behavioural habit formation (Wood & Neal 2007 habit theory).
Linguistic Surface: Automaticity language: 'without thinking', 'on autopilot', 'first thing I do', 'don't even consider alternatives anymore', 'muscle memory'.
Boundary: Not Brand Routine Behaviour (which is time/context-anchored — every Sunday, before bed). Habit Signals are about *non-deliberative use itself*, not its scheduling. Not Habitual Usage — that is consolidated under this signal in the refined library.
Theoretical Anchor: Wood & Neal (2007) habit theory; Verplanken & Aarts (1999) habit and behaviour
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Routine Behaviour, Repurchase Intent, Ecosystem Lock-in
Example 1: I don't even think about it anymore — it's just part of my routine.
Example 2: Reach for it without thinking. Pure muscle memory at this point.
Example 3: Don't consider alternatives anymore. Just use it on autopilot.

Construct: Time-, context-, or schedule-anchored repeated interactions with the product. Distinguished from generic habit by specific temporal or situational triggers ('every morning', 'before bed', 'on Sundays').
Linguistic Surface: Trigger + repetition language: 'every [time period]', 'as part of my [routine]', 'before I [activity]', 'always after [event]'.
Boundary: Not Habit Signals (which are automaticity-anchored, not schedule-anchored). Brand Routine Behaviour requires a *named trigger*.
Theoretical Anchor: Wood, Quinn & Kashy (2002) habits in everyday life; Neal et al. (2012) habitual cues
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Habit Signals, Experience Satisfaction
Example 1: Every Sunday morning I use it to plan my week.
Example 2: Part of my pre-bed routine for two years now.
Example 3: Always run the cleaning mode every Saturday. Just how I do it.

Construct: Recognition of switching costs created by integration with the brand's broader ecosystem of products, services, or data. Captures structural rather than emotional loyalty (Burnham, Frels & Mahajan 2003 switching costs).
Linguistic Surface: Integration-as-barrier language: 'all my [data/files] are in their [service]', 'too invested in the ecosystem', 'switching would mean losing [X]', 'stuck with them because of [integration]'.
Boundary: Not Ecosystem Advantage (Competitive Energy — which is comparative *praise* of integration). Ecosystem Lock-in describes *structural switching cost*, often neutral or grudging in tone.
Theoretical Anchor: Burnham, Frels & Mahajan (2003) switching cost typology; Shapiro & Varian (1999) lock-in
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Habit Signals, Migration Signals, Ecosystem Advantage
Example 1: All my files are in their cloud — I'm stuck.
Example 2: Too invested in the ecosystem to switch now even if I wanted to.
Example 3: Honestly the only reason I haven't switched is the data migration nightmare.

Construct: Multi-year, story-form accounts of ownership emphasising duration and continued function. Indicates both reliability evidence and high attachment (Belk 1988 extended self via possessions).
Linguistic Surface: Multi-year ownership references: 'still using my [year] model', '[duration] and going strong', 'bought this in [year] and...'.
Boundary: Not Product Reliability (which can be short-term consistency). Long-term Ownership Narratives specifically frame *multi-year* tenure and often carry identity-adjacent emotional weight.
Theoretical Anchor: Belk (1988) extended self; Fournier (1998) consumer-brand relationship duration
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Product Reliability, Repurchase Intent, Identity Signals
Example 1: My original unit from 2018 is still going strong today.
Example 2: Ten years with the same model. Still works like day one.
Example 3: Bought this in 2015 and it's outlasted three of my other gadgets.

Construct: Active, self-initiated promotion of the brand to others — going beyond passive satisfaction to evangelism. Operationalised in Reichheld's (2003) NPS framework as the highest-value customer behaviour.
Linguistic Surface: Promotion declarations: 'I tell everyone', 'always recommend', 'preaching about this brand', 'spreading the word'.
Boundary: Not Brand Recommendation (which is a single, direct recommendation utterance). Not Word-of-mouth Promotion (which narrates *received* word-of-mouth). Brand Advocacy is self-reported active evangelism.
Theoretical Anchor: Reichheld (2003) NPS; Anderson (1998) WOM intensity
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Recommendation, Word-of-mouth Promotion, Repurchase Intent
Example 1: I tell everyone I know to switch to this brand.
Example 2: Yeah, I'm that annoying friend who won't shut up about it.
Example 3: Constantly preaching the gospel of this brand to anyone who'll listen.

Construct: A direct, prescriptive utterance recommending the product to an audience. Single-act expression of advocacy rather than self-declaration of evangelism.
Linguistic Surface: Prescriptive direct address: 'get this one', 'go with [Brand]', 'you should buy', 'do yourself a favour and get [Brand]'.
Boundary: Not Brand Advocacy (which is *meta-declaration* about repeated advocacy). Brand Recommendation is the *act* of recommending in the current utterance.
Theoretical Anchor: Reichheld (2003) NPS; Westbrook (1987) post-purchase communication
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Advocacy, Word-of-mouth Promotion, Experience Satisfaction
Example 1: If you're looking for a laptop in this range, get this one. Period.
Example 2: Go with [Brand]. Don't even think about the others.
Example 3: Just buy it. You won't regret it.

Construct: Narrated peer-to-peer transmission of brand information — either receiving or passing along — typically retrospective. Captures the organic information flow that fuels brand growth (Berger 2014 WOM research).
Linguistic Surface: Transmission narration: 'my friend recommended', 'heard about it from', 'told my brother about', 'someone at work mentioned'.
Boundary: Not Brand Recommendation (which is the *act* of recommending). Not Brand Advocacy (which is self-reported evangelism). Word-of-mouth Promotion narrates *the transmission event*.
Theoretical Anchor: Berger (2014) WOM mechanisms; Brown & Reingen (1987) tie strength; Anderson (1998)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Recommendation, Brand Discovery, Brand Advocacy
Example 1: My neighbour recommended this to me and now I see why.
Example 2: Heard about it from a colleague who swears by it.
Example 3: Told my brother about it last month, now he owns one too.

Construct: Use of *formalised, codified* mechanisms for advocacy — referral codes, links, programs, affiliate schemes. Indicates the consumer has crossed from organic advocacy into structured promotion, often with mutual incentive.
Linguistic Surface: Codified-advocacy language: 'use my referral code', 'link in bio', 'use my code for [discount]', 'sign up through me for'.
Boundary: Not Brand Advocacy (organic). Not Brand Recommendation (uncodified). Referral Behaviour involves a *named instrument* of referral.
Theoretical Anchor: Schmitt, Skiera & Van den Bulte (2011) referral programs; Verlegh et al. (2013)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Advocacy, Brand Recommendation
Example 1: Use my referral code RAJ20 for a discount on your first order.
Example 2: Sign up through my link — we both get 10% off.
Example 3: Drop a comment and I'll DM you my referral.

Construct: Active rebuttal of negative claims about the brand in social contexts. A behavioural marker of deep psychological investment in the brand (Cheng et al. 2012 brand defense research).
Linguistic Surface: Counter-criticism language: 'people are being unfair', 'that's not my experience', 'mine doesn't have that problem', 'the hate is overblown'.
Boundary: Not Counter-argument Signals (which is presenting an *alternative perspective* on a complaint, more neutral). Brand Defense is active rebuttal *on behalf of the brand*.
Theoretical Anchor: Cheng, White & Chaplin (2012) brand defense; Bhattacharya & Sen (2003) identification
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Counter-argument Signals, Brand Justification, Identity Signals
Example 1: People are being so unfair — I've never had that issue with mine.
Example 2: The hate online is way overblown. Actually a great product.
Example 3: Don't believe the bad reviews. Mine has been flawless.

Construct: Providing a balanced or alternative perspective in response to a complaint — acknowledging the complaint while offering context or alternative framing. More neutral and less protective than Brand Defense.
Linguistic Surface: Concession + alternative framing: 'I see your point but', 'fair, but for most people', 'true, though [context]', 'understand that, however'.
Boundary: Not Brand Defense (which is full rebuttal). Counter-argument Signals concede partially while reframing — a more measured advocacy posture.
Theoretical Anchor: O'Keefe (1999) two-sided messages and persuasion; Cialdini (1984) social proof framing
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Defense, Brand Justification
Example 1: I see your point, but for most people this isn't actually a problem.
Example 2: Fair concern — though in my use case I've never hit that limit.
Example 3: True for power users. For the average buyer it's totally fine.

Construct: Explaining *why* the brand is the right choice for the speaker personally — reasoning-out-loud about their continued loyalty. Indicates conscious commitment rather than habitual default.
Linguistic Surface: Reasoning for choice: 'I stick with them because', 'the reason I stay is', 'what keeps me with this brand is'.
Boundary: Not Brand Defense (rebuttal). Not Cost Justification (which is specifically price-anchored). Brand Justification is broader self-explanation of loyalty.
Theoretical Anchor: Festinger (1957) cognitive dissonance reduction; Oliver (1999) affective loyalty
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Repurchase Intent, Brand Advocacy, Identity Signals
Example 1: I stick with them because their customer service is unmatched.
Example 2: The reason I stay loyal is the build quality. Period.
Example 3: What keeps me with this brand is honestly the consistency.

Construct: Statements where the brand expresses or extends the *individual's* sense of self. The consumer uses brand affiliation as a self-descriptor. Reflects identity-level fusion (Belk 1988 extended self; Escalas & Bettman 2005 brand-self connection).
Linguistic Surface: Self-identifying with brand: 'as a [Brand] person', 'I'm a [Brand] guy/girl', 'I am the kind of person who uses [Brand]'.
Boundary: Not Community Identity (which is *group* membership). Not Brand Belonging (which is affective bond with the community). Identity Signals are *individual* self-categorisation.
Theoretical Anchor: Belk (1988) extended self; Escalas & Bettman (2005) brand-self connection; Aaker (1997) brand personality
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Community Identity, Brand Belonging, Brand Advocacy
Example 1: As a [Brand] user, I really appreciate the design aesthetic.
Example 2: I'm a [Brand] guy through and through. Always have been.
Example 3: Being a [Brand] customer is honestly part of who I am.

Construct: Affective emotional bond with the *community* of users around the brand. Distinct from identity because it is socially-anchored rather than self-anchored. Reflects communal brand affiliation (Muniz & O'Guinn 2001).
Linguistic Surface: Community-affect language: 'feel like they understand me', 'feel at home with these users', 'this is my crowd', 'finally found my people'.
Boundary: Not Community Identity (explicit membership claim). Not Identity Signals (individual). Brand Belonging is affective bond *with the community*.
Theoretical Anchor: Muniz & O'Guinn (2001) brand community; Bagozzi & Dholakia (2006) consumer subcultures
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Community Identity, Identity Signals, Tribe Language
Example 1: I feel like they really understand people like me.
Example 2: Finally found my crowd in the user community.
Example 3: Honestly this community feels like home.

Construct: Explicit reference to membership in the brand's user community — declarations of being part of the group. Distinguished from Brand Belonging by being an explicit membership claim rather than affective resonance.
Linguistic Surface: Membership declarations: 'part of the [Brand] community', 'fellow [Brand] users', 'one of us', 'member of [Brand] tribe'.
Boundary: Not Brand Belonging (affect). Not Tribe Language (which is *evidence* of membership through jargon, not declaration). Community Identity is explicit membership statement.
Theoretical Anchor: Muniz & O'Guinn (2001) brand community markers; Schau, Muniz & Arnould (2009) communities
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Belonging, Tribe Language, Identity Signals
Example 1: Proud to be part of the [Brand] community.
Example 2: Fellow [Brand] users will get what I mean here.
Example 3: Joined the user forum last year — best community I've been part of.

Construct: The use of in-group jargon, slang, or shorthand specific to the brand's user base — evidence of community membership through linguistic markers rather than explicit declaration. A behavioural indicator of identity fusion.
Linguistic Surface: In-group vocabulary, model nicknames, internal abbreviations, references that only existing users would understand, parenthetical 'fellow users know' tags.
Boundary: Not Community Identity (which is explicit declaration). Tribe Language is *linguistic surface evidence* of community membership — the speaker may not be explicitly identifying as a member, but their vocabulary does.
Theoretical Anchor: Muniz & O'Guinn (2001) brand community markers; Kozinets (2001) Star Trek tribe research
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Community Identity, Brand Belonging, Identity Signals
Example 1: Just finished my daily sync — fellow users know what I mean.
Example 2: Running it on Pro tier with the usual mods. IYKYK.
Example 3: Switched to the build everyone's been raving about in the subreddit.

Construct: Direct, whole-brand head-to-head comparison between two or more named brands. The most general competitive signal — the consumer is treating brands as comparable units of evaluation.
Linguistic Surface: Brand-A vs Brand-B framing: 'Brand X or Brand Y?', 'comparing Brand X and Brand Y', 'between Brand X and Brand Y', 'which is better — [Brand] or [Brand]?'.
Boundary: Not Feature/Price/Performance/Ecosystem Comparison (which are sub-comparisons on specific dimensions). Brand Comparison is the *whole-brand* level. Note: a single utterance often co-fires Brand Comparison with one or more sub-comparison signals (multi-label).
Theoretical Anchor: Shocker et al. (1991) consideration set; Pechmann & Stewart (1990) comparative advertising
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Comparison, Price Comparison, Shortlisting Signals
Example 1: Should I get an Ola S1 or an Ather 450X?
Example 2: Comparing Samsung and OnePlus for my next phone. Thoughts?
Example 3: Between Brand A and Brand B — which is the better buy right now?

Construct: Spec-by-spec, attribute-level comparison between brands. Identifies the specific dimensions on which competitive evaluation is being conducted (Lehmann & Pan 1994 consideration-set drivers).
Linguistic Surface: Feature-mapped comparison: 'Brand X has better [feature], Brand Y has better [other feature]', 'comparing the [features] across both'.
Boundary: Not Brand Comparison (whole-brand). Feature Comparison is *attribute-level*. Note: co-fires frequently with Brand Comparison.
Theoretical Anchor: Lehmann & Pan (1994); Bettman et al. (1998) attribute-based comparison
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Comparison, Performance Comparison, Pros vs Cons Evaluation
Example 1: Brand A has a better screen, but Brand B has way better battery.
Example 2: Camera-wise the rival wins, but everything else tilts to this brand.
Example 3: Feature for feature, they're close — except the speakers, where this brand crushes.

Construct: Comparison of prices across competing brands or products for an equivalent offering. Indicates active competitive shopping and price-anchored consideration.
Linguistic Surface: Cross-brand price language: 'Brand X is $50 cheaper than Brand Y', 'cheapest in the segment', 'most expensive among the lot', 'price-matched against [competitor]'.
Boundary: Not Price Perception (Value Elasticity — single-brand internal feel). Price Comparison is explicitly cross-brand. Note: Price Comparison can also fire Competitor Value Criticism if framed negatively.
Theoretical Anchor: Lichtenstein, Ridgway & Netemeyer (1993) price perception in comparison
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Comparison, Competitor Value Criticism, Value for Money
Example 1: This one is $50 cheaper than the equivalent from the competitor.
Example 2: Among the four I'm considering, this is by far the cheapest.
Example 3: The rival is charging 30% more for basically the same thing.

Construct: Comparison of the integrated 'universes' of competing brands — the breadth and quality of related products, services, and integrations. Reflects ecosystem-as-decision-driver, increasingly common in tech and durables.
Linguistic Surface: Universe-comparison language: 'the [Brand] ecosystem vs the [Brand] ecosystem', 'integration across [Brand] is better', 'switching ecosystems is a pain'.
Boundary: Not Feature Comparison (single-product attributes). Not Ecosystem Advantage (which is one-sided superiority claim). Ecosystem Comparison is two-sided weighing of ecosystems.
Theoretical Anchor: Adner (2017) ecosystem competition; Iansiti & Levien (2004) keystone advantage
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Comparison, Ecosystem Advantage, Ecosystem Lock-in
Example 1: The Apple ecosystem is more polished, but Android gives you way more freedom.
Example 2: Their ecosystem of accessories is unmatched, but you pay for it.
Example 3: Comparing the two ecosystems — both have strengths, depends on what you already own.

Construct: Speed, durability, throughput, or other quantifiable performance metric comparisons between brands — often referring to benchmarks, tests, or measurable outcomes.
Linguistic Surface: Benchmark-cited language: 'in benchmarks this brand beats', 'faster than the rival in tests', 'durability tests showed [Brand] is more reliable'.
Boundary: Not Performance Superiority (which is one-sided assertion without benchmarking framework). Performance Comparison cites measurement or empirical contrast.
Theoretical Anchor: Pechmann & Stewart (1990) comparative claims; Lehmann & Pan (1994)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Comparison, Performance Superiority, Research Behaviour
Example 1: In every benchmark I've seen, this brand beats the rival by 15-20%.
Example 2: Durability tests show Brand A holds up way longer than Brand B.
Example 3: Side by side, the rival's render times are 30% slower.

Construct: Measuring the brand against a *named dominant competitor* — the current market leader serves as the reference standard. Indicates aspirational positioning by the brand or evaluative anchoring by the consumer (Tversky & Kahneman 1974 anchoring).
