Linguistic Surface: Leader-as-yardstick language: 'the [Leader] of [category]', 'trying to be the next [Leader]', 'compared to [Leader] this is...'.
Boundary: Not Category Standard References (which uses an *unnamed* category norm, not a named leader). Market Leader Benchmarking explicitly names the dominant player.
Theoretical Anchor: Tversky & Kahneman (1974) anchoring; Ries & Trout (1981) positioning
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Category Standard References, Category Leadership Signals, Brand Comparison
Example 1: It's trying to be the iPhone of the EV space.
Example 2: They want to position themselves as the [Leader] killer.
Example 3: Compared to the market leader, this is 80% as good at 60% of the price.

Construct: Comparison of the brand against an *unnamed* but implicit category norm — what 'most brands in this segment' offer. Reveals the consumer's mental category prototype (Rosch 1978) and expectations of the segment.
Linguistic Surface: Category-norm references: 'most brands at this price', 'standard in this segment', 'everyone else offers [X], why doesn't this?', 'category-standard [feature]'.
Boundary: Not Market Leader Benchmarking (named leader). Category Standard References operates on the *aggregate norm* of the category.
Theoretical Anchor: Rosch (1978) prototype theory; Sujan (1985) category-based affect transfer
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Market Leader Benchmarking, Feature Comparison, Competitive Weakness Signals
Example 1: Most brands in this price range offer 4K — why doesn't this?
Example 2: Category-standard at this segment includes wireless charging. They skipped it.
Example 3: Everyone else in this tier has this feature. Strange omission.

Construct: Retrospective, story-form accounts of moving from one brand to another — typically including duration, reasons, and outcome. Rich diagnostic data on the drivers of churn and acquisition (Bansal & Taylor 2002 service switching).
Linguistic Surface: Retrospective switching stories: 'after [time] of using [Brand A], I finally moved to [Brand B] because...', extended past-tense narration of the switch.
Boundary: Not Switching Signals (which are present-tense intent or shorter declarations). Not Migration Signals (which use territorial/ecosystem-level metaphor). Switching Narratives are *retrospective*.
Theoretical Anchor: Bansal & Taylor (2002) service provider switching; Keaveney (1995) switching antecedents
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Switching Signals, Migration Signals, Competitor Complaints
Example 1: After ten years of using Brand A, I finally made the switch to Brand B last month — and here's why.
Example 2: Was on the rival for five years. Switched in 2023 and never looked back.
Example 3: Long story of why I left Brand X — battery issues, terrible support, then they raised prices.

Construct: Present-tense or imminent declaration of intent to change brands. Distinguished from narratives by being short, action-focused, and forward-looking.
Linguistic Surface: Intent or completed-action statements: 'switching to [Brand]', 'tired of [Brand A], moving to [Brand B]', 'made the switch', 'jumping ship'.
Boundary: Not Brand Switching Narratives (retrospective, long-form). Not Migration Signals (territorial metaphor). Switching Signals are short and declarative.
Theoretical Anchor: Keaveney (1995) service switching; Burnham, Frels & Mahajan (2003) switching costs
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Switching Narratives, Migration Signals, Replacement Intent
Example 1: I'm tired of [Brand A] — switching to [Brand B] this month.
Example 2: Just made the switch. Can already tell I'll never go back.
Example 3: Jumping ship from [Brand A]. Done with them.

Construct: Brand-change language framed using *territorial, ecosystem, or movement metaphors* — implying the change is more structural than a simple swap. Often signals ecosystem-level migration with switching costs.
Linguistic Surface: Territorial movement language: 'moving away from', 'leaving the ecosystem', 'migrating off', 'getting out of [Brand]'s walled garden'.
Boundary: Not Switching Signals (which is product-level). Not Brand Switching Narratives (retrospective). Migration Signals carry ecosystem-level connotation.
Theoretical Anchor: Burnham, Frels & Mahajan (2003) switching costs; Shapiro & Varian (1999) lock-in
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Switching Signals, Ecosystem Lock-in, Brand Switching Narratives
Example 1: Looking to migrate away from legacy software to something modern.
Example 2: Slowly moving my entire workflow off [Brand] this year.
Example 3: Time to leave the walled garden. Migrating to [open alternative].

Construct: Statements about trying a rival's product specifically for the purpose of comparison or evaluation, often while remaining on the current brand. Distinct from full switching — indicates exploration of alternatives without commitment.
Linguistic Surface: Trial-of-rival language: 'tried the competitor', 'bought [Rival] just to see', 'testing both side by side', 'sample of [Rival] for comparison'.
Boundary: Not Switching Signals (which is intent to leave). Not Trial Intent (Demand Gravity — for the *target* brand). Competitor Trial Signals are *exploration* of rivals.
Theoretical Anchor: Bawa & Shoemaker (1987) trial behaviour; Lehmann & Pan (1994) consideration
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Switching Signals, Brand Comparison, Feature Comparison
Example 1: Bought the competitor's version just to see if it's actually better.
Example 2: Testing both side by side for a month before deciding.
Example 3: Picked up the rival as a backup just to compare.

Construct: Assertions that the brand's specific feature is better than competitors' equivalent feature. One-sided claim of competitive advantage at the attribute level.
Linguistic Surface: Superiority assertion at feature level: '[feature] is miles ahead of everyone else', 'best [feature] in the category', 'nothing else comes close on [feature]'.
Boundary: Not Feature Comparison (which is two-sided weighing). Not Performance Superiority (broader, overall-performance level). Feature Superiority Claims are *feature-specific one-sided assertion*.
Theoretical Anchor: Pechmann & Stewart (1990) comparative claims; Wilkie & Farris (1975) comparative advertising
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Performance Superiority, Competitive Advantage Signals, Feature Comparison
Example 1: Their noise cancellation is miles ahead of everyone else.
Example 2: Best camera in this category — nothing else comes close.
Example 3: The keyboard on this is just untouchable. Best in class.

Construct: One-sided assertion that the brand outperforms rivals on overall or core performance — speed, power, throughput, durability. Broader than feature-level superiority.
Linguistic Surface: Overall-performance superiority: 'no contest — choose this brand', 'in raw power, nothing matches', 'fastest in the market'.
Boundary: Not Feature Superiority Claims (feature-specific). Not Performance Comparison (which is two-sided benchmarking). Performance Superiority is one-sided whole-product.
Theoretical Anchor: Pechmann & Stewart (1990) comparative claims
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Superiority Claims, Performance Comparison, Brand Advocacy
Example 1: If you want raw power, there's no contest — choose this brand.
Example 2: Fastest in the market. Period.
Example 3: Nothing else even comes close on overall performance.

Construct: Praise for the brand's integration with its broader ecosystem of products and services, framed as a competitive advantage over rivals. One-sided ecosystem claim.
Linguistic Surface: Integration-advantage language: 'the way it talks to my other [Brand] devices', 'integration is unmatched', 'ecosystem is why I chose [Brand] over rivals'.
Boundary: Not Ecosystem Comparison (two-sided). Not Ecosystem Lock-in (Reinforcement Stability — which is structural switching cost, not competitive praise). Ecosystem Advantage is positive comparative.
Theoretical Anchor: Adner (2017) ecosystem competition; Iansiti & Levien (2004)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Ecosystem Comparison, Ecosystem Lock-in, Competitive Advantage Signals
Example 1: The way it integrates with my other [Brand] devices is why I picked it over the rival.
Example 2: Ecosystem-wise, no one else even comes close to this brand.
Example 3: The integration story is what wins this for me over the competition.

Construct: General one-sided claims that the brand is winning on a specific dimension — not necessarily feature, performance, or ecosystem, but any competitive differentiator (support, warranty, shipping, etc.).
Linguistic Surface: Unique-advantage language: 'only brand that does [X]', 'no one else offers [Y]', 'their [Z] is unmatched'.
Boundary: Not Feature/Performance/Ecosystem Superiority (which are dimension-specific). Competitive Advantage Signals is the catch-all for non-product-feature advantages.
Theoretical Anchor: Porter (1985) competitive advantage; Day & Wensley (1988)
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Feature Superiority Claims, Performance Superiority, Brand Justification
Example 1: This is the only brand that offers 24/7 onsite support.
Example 2: No one else gives you a 5-year warranty in this category.
Example 3: Their return policy alone makes them a no-brainer over the rivals.

Construct: Direct venting about negative experiences with a rival brand. Reveals competitor weakness from the consumer's perspective and indirectly strengthens the focal brand's position.
Linguistic Surface: Negative-experience venting about rival: 'support at [Rival] is terrible', '[Rival] has been awful', 'sick of [Rival]'s issues'.
Boundary: Not Competitor Reliability Issues (which is specifically *doubt about long-term performance*). Competitor Complaints are broader venting, often about service, support, experience.
Theoretical Anchor: Keaveney (1995) switching antecedents; Singh (1988) consumer complaint behaviour
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Competitor Reliability Issues, Brand Switching Narratives, Switching Signals
Example 1: The support at [Rival Brand] is absolutely terrible.
Example 2: Their customer service made me want to throw my device out the window.
Example 3: I'm done with [Rival]'s constant software bugs.

Construct: Doubt or evidence about the rival brand's long-term performance and durability. Often based on reports, reviews, or peer experience rather than personal.
Linguistic Surface: Rival-reliability doubt: 'so many reports of [Rival]'s product failing', '[Rival] doesn't last', 'heard [Rival] units die after [time]'.
Boundary: Not Competitor Complaints (broader venting). Not Reliability Concerns (Choice Architecture — about the focal brand). Competitor Reliability Issues are specifically rival-targeted durability doubt.
Theoretical Anchor: Mitchell (1999) perceived risk; Roselius (1971) risk reduction
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Competitor Complaints, Reliability Concerns, Brand Switching Narratives
Example 1: I've seen so many reports of the rival's product failing after a month.
Example 2: Heard [Rival] units start dying within a year. Not taking that risk.
Example 3: Three of my friends have had their [Rival] devices break. Pattern.

Construct: Assertion that a rival brand is *overpriced* relative to what it delivers — explicit negative value-for-money judgment of the competitor.
Linguistic Surface: Rival-overpriced language: '[Rival] is charging double for the same thing', 'paying [Rival] tax', '[Rival]'s price is a joke', 'overpriced compared to [Focal]'.
Boundary: Not Price Comparison (which is two-sided/neutral). Not Competitor Complaints (broader negative). Competitor Value Criticism is specifically the *value-for-money negative judgment of rival*.
Theoretical Anchor: Zeithaml (1988) perceived value; Thaler (1985) reference price
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Price Comparison, Competitor Complaints, Value for Money
Example 1: The competitor is charging double for basically the same features.
Example 2: Paying the [Rival] tax just for the badge at this point.
Example 3: [Rival] is wildly overpriced compared to what you get.

Construct: Statements positioning the brand as defining or leading the category — others are imitators rather than peers. Reflects strong positioning and category-creator dynamics.
Linguistic Surface: Leadership/originality language: 'everyone else is copying [Brand]', '[Brand] defined this category', 'this is what others are trying to imitate'.
Boundary: Not Market Leader Benchmarking (which uses another brand as the standard for *this* brand). Category Leadership Signals are about *this brand* being the standard.
Theoretical Anchor: Ries & Trout (1981) positioning; Carpenter & Nakamoto (1989) pioneer advantage
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Market Position Signals, Market Leader Benchmarking, Brand Positioning Language
Example 1: Everyone else is just trying to copy what this brand did first.
Example 2: They defined the category. Everyone else is playing catch-up.
Example 3: This is the original — the rest are clones.

Construct: Rallying language behind a smaller, challenger, or non-dominant brand against larger incumbents. Reflects David-vs-Goliath narrative framing and challenger-brand affinity.
Linguistic Surface: Underdog-affection language: 'small startup taking on the giants', 'rooting for the underdog', 'love seeing them beat the big players'.
Boundary: Not Category Leadership Signals (which positions the brand as dominant). Underdog Narratives are about *challenger affinity*.
Theoretical Anchor: Paharia et al. (2011) underdog brand biography; Holt (2004) cultural branding
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Positioning Language, Brand Advocacy, Market Position Signals
Example 1: I love seeing this small startup take on the giants.
Example 2: Rooting for them — David vs Goliath energy.
Example 3: Honestly love that they're the underdog still punching above their weight.

Construct: Observations or assertions about where the brand currently sits in the market hierarchy — niche, mid-tier, premium, leader, declining. Captures positional dynamics.
Linguistic Surface: Position-describing language: 'gone from niche to top', 'used to be mid-tier, now premium', 'falling from the top spot', 'climbing fast'.
Boundary: Not Category Leadership Signals (which specifically asserts leadership). Market Position Signals describe *position movement or status* without necessarily claiming leadership.
Theoretical Anchor: Ries & Trout (1981) positioning; Day (1981) PLC and market position
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Category Leadership Signals, Brand Positioning Language, Demand Momentum Signals
Example 1: They've gone from a niche player to the top of the market in three years.
Example 2: [Brand] is falling from its leadership spot — rivals are catching up fast.
Example 3: Used to be a budget option, now they're firmly mid-premium.

Construct: Consumer's articulation of where the brand 'feels' positioned relative to others — premium, budget, aspirational, accessible. Reveals perceived positioning, which may differ from intended positioning.
Linguistic Surface: Position-feel descriptors: 'budget-friendly alternative', 'premium without trying too hard', 'the [adjective] option compared to'.
Boundary: Not Market Position Signals (which describes objective market hierarchy). Brand Positioning Language is *subjective perceived positioning*.
Theoretical Anchor: Ries & Trout (1981) positioning; Keller (1993) brand image
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Market Position Signals, Underdog Narratives, Identity Signals
Example 1: This feels like the budget-friendly alternative to the premium brands.
Example 2: [Brand] is the premium without trying too hard option.
Example 3: They've got that aspirational-but-accessible vibe going.

Construct: Observer-reported descriptions of accelerating or decelerating collective interest in a brand or category. Captures the temporal derivative of attention — not the level, but the *change*. Anchored in diffusion theory (Rogers 1962) and informational cascade models (Bikhchandani et al. 1992).
Linguistic Surface: Temporal-acceleration language: 'lately everyone is talking about', 'suddenly seeing this everywhere', 'used to be niche, now booming', 'losing momentum'.
Boundary: Not Brand Mention Share (which is *snapshot* share, not change). Not Brand Salience (which is individual retrieval). Demand Momentum is observer-reported aggregate change.
Theoretical Anchor: Rogers (1962) Diffusion of Innovations; Bikhchandani, Hirshleifer & Welch (1992) cascades
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Topic Emergence, Feature Trend Signals, Product Launch Interest
Example 1: Everyone seems to be talking about this brand lately.
Example 2: Suddenly I'm seeing this product all over my feed — wasn't a thing a month ago.
Example 3: Used to be a niche brand, now they're absolutely booming.

Construct: Anticipation, speculation, or excitement around an upcoming product release. Indicates forward-looking demand pull at the market level, often correlated with category-creator and early-adopter dynamics.
Linguistic Surface: Anticipation language: 'can't wait for the reveal', 'leaks suggest', 'launching next month', 'rumours about the new model'.
Boundary: Not Product Lifecycle Signals (Demand Gravity — which is about *current* product's lifecycle position). Product Launch Interest is *forward-looking* toward upcoming launches.
Theoretical Anchor: Rogers (1962) innovators and early adopters; Holak & Lehmann (1990) innovation adoption
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Demand Momentum Signals, Topic Emergence, Innovation Value
Example 1: Can't wait for the reveal next Tuesday — fingers crossed for the rumoured features.
Example 2: Leaks suggest the new model will have [feature]. Big if true.
Example 3: Counting down to the launch. Already saved up for day-one purchase.

Construct: Sudden appearance of a new theme, concern, or discussion topic in category conversation. Indicates an emerging issue or opportunity — often the leading edge of a trend before it consolidates into mainstream conversation.
Linguistic Surface: Emergence-marking language: 'suddenly everyone is asking about [topic]', 'new thing everyone's discussing', 'this came out of nowhere'.
Boundary: Not Feature Trend Signals (which is specifically about *features*). Topic Emergence is broader — themes, concerns, debates.
Theoretical Anchor: Burt (2004) structural holes and idea emergence; Granovetter (1973) weak ties
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Demand Momentum Signals, Feature Trend Signals, Brand Mention Share
Example 1: Suddenly everyone is asking about the battery safety of these models.
Example 2: This sustainability angle just emerged in the last month — wasn't on anyone's radar before.
Example 3: New topic everyone is discussing: the right-to-repair issue with these brands.

Construct: Increasing volume of discussion about a specific product feature over time. Indicates rising expectations or demand for that feature in the category — leading indicator of feature-level category evolution.
Linguistic Surface: Feature-trend language: 'more and more people asking for [feature]', '[feature] is becoming a must-have', 'demand for [feature] is growing'.
Boundary: Not Topic Emergence (broader). Not Feature Curiosity (Demand Gravity — individual-level interest). Feature Trend Signals are observer-reported aggregate feature interest growth.
Theoretical Anchor: Rogers (1962); Garcia & Calantone (2002) innovation typology
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Topic Emergence, Demand Momentum Signals, Category Standard References
Example 1: More and more people are asking for a dark mode in the reviews.
Example 2: [Feature] is becoming a must-have — every new launch has it now.
Example 3: Demand for [feature] has exploded in the last six months across forums.

Construct: Alignment between the brand's stated or perceived values (sustainability, ethics, inclusivity, craftsmanship) and the consumer's personal value system. Drives values-based brand affinity (Holt 2004 cultural branding; Belk 1988 extended self).
Linguistic Surface: Values-alignment language: 'their values match mine', 'they stand for what I believe in', 'finally a brand that gets it', 'aligned with my principles'.
Boundary: Not Identity Signals (Reinforcement Stability — which is individual self-categorisation). Values Congruence is specifically about *value-system alignment* between brand and consumer.
Theoretical Anchor: Holt (2004) cultural branding; Belk (1988); Sirgy (1982) self-congruity theory
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Ethical & Sustainability Signals, Identity Signals, Brand Justification
Example 1: Their values match mine — that's why I'll keep buying from them.
Example 2: Finally a brand that stands for what I actually believe in.
Example 3: Their principles are completely out of step with mine. Won't support them.

Construct: Consumer evaluation of the brand through an ethical, environmental, or sustainability lens — sourcing, labour practices, carbon footprint, packaging, animal welfare. Increasingly material to purchase decisions (Devinney, Auger & Eckhardt 2010 myth of the ethical consumer).
Linguistic Surface: Sustainability/ethics language: 'eco-friendly', 'sustainable materials', 'ethical sourcing', 'carbon neutral', 'fair trade', 'cruelty-free', 'greenwashing'.
Boundary: Not Values Congruence (broader value-system alignment). Ethical & Sustainability Signals are specifically about *ethics and environmental* dimensions.
Theoretical Anchor: Devinney et al. (2010); Carrigan & Attalla (2001) ethical consumer; Peattie (2010) green marketing
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Values Congruence, Brand Justification, Brand Positioning Language
Example 1: Love that they use sustainable materials and disclose their carbon footprint.
Example 2: Their sourcing practices are a mess. Wouldn't buy from them.
Example 3: Classic greenwashing — they put a leaf on the box and call it eco-friendly.

Construct: Evaluation of the brand through the lens of inclusion, representation, social positions, or political associations. The brand is judged for its stances, partnerships, or perceived political alignment (Holt 2004; Banet-Weiser 2012 branded politics).
Linguistic Surface: Identity-political language: 'inclusive', 'tone-deaf', 'on the right side of', 'cancelled', 'supporting [cause]', 'taking a stance'.
Boundary: Not Values Congruence (broader values). Not Ethical & Sustainability Signals (which are specifically ethics/environment). Identity Politics Signals concern *social-political* dimensions.
Theoretical Anchor: Banet-Weiser (2012) authentic branding; Holt (2004) cultural branding
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Values Congruence, Brand Defense, Brand Justification
Example 1: Glad to see them taking a stance on this. Buying more from them.
Example 2: Their latest campaign is honestly tone-deaf. Stepping away from this brand.
Example 3: Finally a brand that represents people like me in their marketing.

Construct: Evaluation of the brand through the lens of country of origin, regional identity, or local manufacturing — typically with affective pride or national-preference framing. Captures country-of-origin effects (Bilkey & Nes 1982; Verlegh & Steenkamp 1999).
Linguistic Surface: Origin-pride language: 'made in India', 'supporting local', 'proud to back a [country] brand', 'homegrown', 'desi brand', 'finally something built here'.
Boundary: Not Underdog Narratives (Competitive Energy — which is about challenger size, not origin). Regional & National Pride Signals are specifically *origin-based*.
Theoretical Anchor: Bilkey & Nes (1982) country of origin effects; Verlegh & Steenkamp (1999) COO meta-analysis
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Justification, Values Congruence, Underdog Narratives
Example 1: Proud to support a homegrown Indian brand. Switching from the foreign one.
Example 2: Finally a desi brand competing with the global players. Backing them.
Example 3: Don't trust anything not made in [country]. Sticking with local.

Construct: Resonance of the brand with a specific cultural moment, trend, or zeitgeist — festivals, viral moments, cultural shifts, generational identity markers. Indicates the brand has tapped into a temporal cultural current (Holt 2004 iconic brands).
Linguistic Surface: Cultural-moment language: 'perfect for [festival/moment]', 'captures the [generation] vibe', 'on-trend for what's happening now', 'right brand for [cultural moment]'.
Boundary: Not Brand Positioning Language (which is general position perception). Cultural Moment Alignment ties the brand to a *specific cultural moment or trend*.
Theoretical Anchor: Holt (2004) cultural branding and iconic brands; Arnould & Thompson (2005) CCT
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Positioning Language, Demand Momentum Signals, Identity Signals
Example 1: Honestly perfect for the post-pandemic work-from-home moment.
Example 2: This brand just captures the Gen Z vibe in a way the others don't.
Example 3: Right brand for the cost-of-living moment we're in.

Construct: Evaluation anchored in the brand's history, craft tradition, or perceived authenticity versus inauthentic alternatives. Reflects authenticity-as-cultural-capital (Beverland 2005 brand authenticity; Holt 2002 authentic branding).
Linguistic Surface: Heritage/authenticity language: 'old-school quality', '[X] years of craft', 'the real deal', 'authentic', 'unlike the new copycats', 'they actually know what they're doing'.
Boundary: Not Category Leadership Signals (which is competitive market position). Heritage & Authenticity Signals are about *history, craft, and authenticity*.
Theoretical Anchor: Beverland (2005) brand authenticity; Holt (2002) commercial mythmaking
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Brand Justification, Values Congruence, Long-term Ownership Narratives
Example 1: Old-school quality. They've been making these for 60 years and it shows.
Example 2: Authentic craft brand — none of the mass-produced nonsense.
Example 3: They've completely lost the heritage feel since the rebrand. Inauthentic now.

Construct: Negative cultural response to brand actions perceived as misaligned with consumer values, cultural norms, or political moment. Captures cancel-culture dynamics and reputational shock events (Banet-Weiser 2018).
Linguistic Surface: Backlash language: 'cancelling [Brand]', 'won't buy after [incident]', 'they crossed a line', 'boycotting', 'this is the last straw'.
Boundary: Not Brand Defense (Reinforcement Stability — which protects the brand). Cultural Backlash Signals are *active rejection* of the brand for cultural/value reasons.
Theoretical Anchor: Banet-Weiser (2018) Empowered; Klein (1999) No Logo
Multi-Label Co-occurrence: Switching Signals, Values Congruence, Identity Politics Signals
Example 1: Cancelling this brand after their CEO's comments. Done.
Example 2: Won't be buying from them after the way they handled that incident.
Example 3: Last straw for me — boycotting them and so are most of my friends.
